notes.anchor: we are pressed by so many and such great troubles that no one but a perfect fool ought to hope for any relief
notes.note: Written in late 47 or early 46 BC, after Caesar's victory at Pharsalus had ended the Republic that Cicero and Varro had served on Pompey's side; both men were pardoned but politically powerless under Caesar's emerging dictatorship, and this gloom over the state's ruin runs through the whole Varro correspondence.

notes.anchor: I have made my peace with my old friends --- that is, with our books
notes.note: Excluded from public life under Caesar, Cicero turned in these years to writing the great philosophical and rhetorical works of 46--44 BC; the 'old friends' are his books and studies, which he had neglected while embroiled in the civil war.

notes.anchor: you, who held to it, were the wiser man of the two
notes.note: M. Terentius Varro (116--27 BC) was Rome's most learned scholar, author of hundreds of works; Cicero contrasts Varro's steadier devotion to study with his own deeper entanglement in politics and the failed Pompeian cause.

Tusculum (in the Alban hills southeast of Rome) and Cumae (on the Bay of Naples) were sites of Cicero's country villas; such retreats were where he and his learned friends could meet for the leisured study (otium) prized by educated Romans.
